text: Three years ago, he was found to have advanced ascending colon cancer infiltrating the liver in the S-six region. He underwent open-right hemicolectomy and partial resection of the S-six region.
He was found to have liver metastasis, so he underwent laparoscopic partial liver lateral segmentectomy one year and three months after the initial surgery after another chemotherapy.
Eleven months after the second surgery, he had a swollen paraurtic lymph node, and it was diagnosed as a metastasis of the colon cancer by PET-CT.
He underwent laparoscopic paraurtic lymphadenectomy twenty-one months after the second surgery.
The layer containing the target lesion could be safely rejected even with the laparoscopic surgery, as it had never been detached. This is why we chose a laparoscopic surgery.
The surgeon carefully dissects severe adhesions between the abdominal wall and the omentum, separating these structures to proceed with the ileocolic resection.
In the prior two surgeries, we had not peeled off the layer behind the duodenum, so we could perform cohormabilization.
The surgeon dissects the mesenteric attachment, leaving the parotid lymph node tissue intact around the inferior vena cava.
The surgeon exposes the inferior vena cava, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the left renal vein as it flows into the inferior vena cava, located behind the duodenum and the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon dissects around the left renal vein, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and associated nerve tissue located superior to the vein. A metastatic lymph node, approximately two centimeters in size, is exposed during the procedure.
The metastatic lymph node is visually surrounded by the pancreatic parenchyma, the polar vein, the right crux of the diaphragm, and the liver caudate lobe.
The surgeon clips and cuts the feeding artery of the metastatic lymph node, successfully excising the lymph node with minimal bleeding observed.
In the metastatic lymph node, only CK-VII was immunostained, but neither CDX-II nor CK-XX was immunostained. It was more probably differentiated than the primary cancer of the ascending colon.
He had no severe postoperative complications but had a little lymphuria and stayed at the hospital for eleven days after the surgery.
He has had no recurrences for two years and four months after the laparoscopic parotid lymphadenectomy with no chemotherapy after the surgery.

Even in patients with multiple previous abdominal surgeries, laparoscopic surgery may be useful if the target lesion is in a previously undetected layer. Importantly, patients with a history of multiple laparotomy may be increasing in the future due to improvements in the results of systemic therapy.
